lesson_title: Sharing global constants across multiple files (using inline variables)

cards.front: What happens when you define a const variable in a header file that is included in multiple .cpp files?
cards.back: Each .cpp file gets its own independent copy of the variable (because const has internal linkage by default). This wastes memory.
cards.source: 7.10 - Sharing global constants across multiple files

cards.front: What are two methods for sharing const global variables across files?
cards.back:
• Use extern const (pre-C++17 method)
  • Use inline const or inline constexpr (C++17 and later)
cards.source: 7.10 - Sharing global constants across multiple files

cards.text: To share a constant across files using inline (C++17), define it in a header as {{c1::inline constexpr double gravity{ 9.8 };}}
cards.source: 7.10 - Sharing global constants across multiple files

cards.question: How do you share a constant using extern (pre-C++17)?
cards.answer:
// constants.cpp
extern const int maxStudents{ 30 };

// constants.h
extern const int maxStudents;
cards.explanation: Define the constant with 'extern const' in a .cpp file and forward declare it with 'extern const' in a header file.
cards.source: 7.10 - Sharing global constants across multiple files

cards.front: What is the preferred modern method for sharing constants across files?
cards.back: Use inline constexpr variables in a header file (C++17 and later). This is simpler and ensures only one copy exists.
cards.source: 7.10 - Sharing global constants across multiple files

cards.front: Why prefer constexpr over const for global constants?
cards.back: Constexpr constants can be used in contexts that require compile-time constants (like array sizes) and are optimized better by the compiler.
cards.source: 7.10 - Sharing global constants across multiple files

cards.front: What advantage do inline variables have over extern const?
cards.back: Inline variables can be defined directly in the header file (no separate .cpp file needed), are guaranteed to have only one instance, and are simpler to use.
cards.source: 7.10 - Sharing global constants across multiple files

cards.text: Global constants should be placed in a {{c1::namespace}} to avoid naming conflicts and to make their purpose clear.
cards.source: 7.10 - Sharing global constants across multiple files

cards.question: What's the best way to organize global constants?
cards.answer:
// constants.h
namespace constants
{
    inline constexpr double pi{ 3.14159 };
    inline constexpr double gravity{ 9.8 };
}
cards.explanation: Put inline constexpr constants in a namespace within a header file. This is clean, modern, and avoids naming conflicts.
cards.source: 7.10 - Sharing global constants across multiple files

cards.front: Why is it a problem to have multiple copies of a const variable when included from a header?
cards.back: It wastes memory (each translation unit gets its own copy) and if the variable is large or complex, it can significantly increase compilation time and executable size.
cards.source: 7.10 - Sharing global constants across multiple files

cards.explanation:
In modern C++ (C++17+), use inline constexpr variables in a header file:
  • Define once in a header inside a namespace
  • No separate .cpp file needed
  • Only one copy exists across all translation units
  • Can be used in compile-time contexts
  • Simple and clean
cards.source: 7.10 - Sharing global constants across multiple files

cards.text: The modern C++17 way to share constants is to use {{c1::inline constexpr}} variables in a {{c2::header file}}.
cards.source: 7.10 - Sharing global constants across multiple files

cards.text: Prefer {{c1::constexpr}} over const for global constants because constexpr can be used in {{c2::compile-time contexts}}.
cards.source: 7.10 - Sharing global constants across multiple files

cards.text: Place global constants in a {{c1::namespace}} to avoid naming conflicts: namespace constants { inline constexpr double {{c2::pi}}{ 3.14159 }; }
cards.source: 7.10 - Sharing global constants across multiple files

cards.text: Pre-C++17 method to share constants: define with {{c1::extern const}} in a .cpp file and forward declare with {{c2::extern const}} in a header.
cards.source: 7.10 - Sharing global constants across multiple files
